notes.anchor: M. CICERO IMP. S. D. M. CATONI
notes.note: IMP. = imperator, the title by which troops acclaimed a victorious general; Cicero's soldiers had hailed him so after the Amanus campaign, and the salutation flags the very claim this letter presses on Cato. S. D. = salutem dicit, 'sends greeting'.

notes.anchor: in accordance with a decree of the Senate
notes.note: Cicero's levy of recalled veterans (evocati) and allied auxiliaries rested on a senatorial decree authorizing the eastern commanders to raise troops against the Parthian threat of 51 BC.

notes.anchor: the Parthians had crossed into Syria
notes.note: The Parthian empire beyond the Euphrates was Rome's great eastern rival; their invasion of Syria after the disaster at Carrhae (53 BC) is the looming menace behind Cicero's whole governorship and this dispatch.

notes.anchor: there are two passes from Syria into Cilicia
notes.note: Mount Amanus is the range dividing Cilicia from Syria; the two passes are the Amanic and Syrian Gates, narrow defiles that a small force could hold, which is why Cicero judged Cilicia easily defensible but Cappadocia exposed.

notes.anchor: whose safety I held under commendation from the Senate at your motion
notes.note: Ariobarzanes III, Roman client king of Cappadocia; the Senate had specially commended his protection to Cicero on a motion of Cato's, which is why Cicero stresses Cato's personal stake in the king's preservation.

M. Calpurnius Bibulus, Caesar's former consular colleague, was now governor of neighbouring Syria. His arrival at Antioch relieved Cicero, who as governor of Cilicia held a parallel but separate command and a sometimes-uneasy rivalry with him.

notes.anchor: removing from that mountain its perpetual enemy
notes.note: Amanus harboured autonomous brigand tribes who raided both provinces; clearing the mountain was the pretext and substance of the campaign that earned Cicero his imperator-acclamation.

notes.anchor: the town of Pindenissus, of the Free Cilicians
notes.note: The Eleutherocilices ('Free Cilicians') were highland peoples of Amanus who had never submitted to any king; the fifty-seven-day siege and capture of their fortress Pindenissus was the chief military exploit of Cicero's command.

notes.anchor: who were harbouring runaway slaves and were waiting in eager expectation of the Parthians' coming
notes.note: Cicero justifies the assault on Pindenissus as a matter of imperial prestige (existimatio imperi): the town sheltered fugitives and hoped for a Parthian advance, so its crushing would overawe other disaffected communities.

notes.anchor: you would not vote a thanksgiving to a certain most distinguished and excellent man
notes.note: A supplicatio was a public thanksgiving voted by the Senate, opening the temples for prayers; in a victorious general's name it was a high honour and the usual prelude to a triumph. The unnamed man whom Cato opposed is Pompey; Cicero recalls Cato's scruples to flatter the consistency he now asks Cato to extend to him.
Cicero's enemy is P. Clodius Pulcher, killed by T. Annius Milo's men in 52 BC; Cato had backed the defence of Milo, which Cicero counts among Cato's services to him against their common foe.

notes.anchor: I afterwards desired to become augur
notes.note: The augurate was a lifelong priesthood charged with reading the auspices, one of Rome's most coveted honours; Cicero was co-opted into the college in 53 BC after the death of the younger Crassus.

notes.anchor: your two greatest clients in particular --- the island of Cyprus and the kingdom of Cappadocia
notes.note: Cato had annexed and organized Cyprus as a Roman province in 58-56 BC, and had championed Cappadocia's king in the Senate; Cicero invokes these as Cato's 'clients' whose testimony will vouch for Cicero's just provincial administration.
